BUS 423 • Quantitative Investing • NC State University

Keystone Quality Fund

Quality Growth at a Reasonable Price • A Quantitative Factor Strategy

Abby Clark April 2026 In-Sample: 1970–2003 • OOS: 2004–2023
01The Thesis

The Thesis

Why quality growth at a reasonable price should outperform — and how we built a systematic strategy around it.

11.22%
Full-Period CAGR
$275.33
Strategy Final Dollar
30
Stocks Held
63.9%
Monthly Win Rate

The Composite Signal

Score = be_me  +  ni_me  +  ret_12_1  −  oaccruals
Each variable is winsorized at the 1% / 99% tails and standardized to a z-score before summing. Stocks are ranked from highest to lowest composite score. The oaccruals signal is subtracted because high accruals predict underperformance.
Core idea: Buy stocks that are cheap relative to book value and earnings, have strong recent price momentum, and generate earnings backed by real cash rather than accounting accruals. This combination targets the intersection of value, profitability, and quality.

Four Factor Pillars

B/M
Value
be_me

Book-to-market equity. High values signal stocks trading below accounting value. Fama & French (1992) showed value stocks persistently outperform growth stocks.

E/P
Profitability
ni_me

Net income divided by market equity — the inverse of P/E. Purchasing earnings cheaply relative to price. Novy-Marx (2013) showed profitability strongly predicts future returns.

12m
Momentum
ret_12_1

Past return from month t−12 to t−1, skipping the last month to avoid short-term reversal. Jegadeesh & Titman (1993): one of the strongest anomalies ever documented.

OA
Quality
oaccruals (−)

Operating accruals isolate non-cash components of earnings. Sloan (1996): firms with high accruals systematically underperform as paper earnings revert to cash reality.

Portfolio Construction

1

Filter Universe

Remove micro-caps: stocks in the bottom 20% of market equity each month are excluded to avoid liquidity and trading cost issues.

2

Rank into Size Buckets

Standardize each signal using z-scores, then rank all remaining stocks. Divide into three size-based buckets using market equity to ensure representation across the capitalization spectrum.

3

Select Top 10 Per Bucket

Choose the 10 highest-scoring stocks from each bucket (3 buckets × 10 = 30 stocks total), equally weighted.

4

Validate & Finalize Factors

Run Fama-MacBeth cross-sectional regressions monthly to confirm each variable's statistical significance. Drop or add variables based on evidence — this led to dropping an earnings yield version and refining to the final four-factor signal.

5

Hold & Rebalance Monthly

Hold the 30 equal-weighted positions for one month, then re-score and rebalance. Monthly rebalancing captures factor signal updates at the cost of turnover.

Decile Monotonicity — Does Sorting on Each Factor Work?

Before building the composite, we verify each factor individually: when stocks are sorted into ten buckets by each signal, do higher buckets earn higher average returns? All four factors show the expected monotonic pattern in-sample.

Book-to-Market (be_me): Top-decile value stocks earn meaningfully more than cheap-looking growth stocks, confirming the value premium in this universe.
Net Income / Market (ni_me): High-earnings-yield stocks outperform, consistent with the profitability literature and Novy-Marx (2013).
Momentum (ret_12_1): Strong prior-year winners continue outperforming. The skip-month removes the noisy reversal at the 1-month horizon.
Accruals (oaccruals, inverted): Low-accrual firms outperform high-accrual firms, because cash-backed earnings are more persistent than accrual-based earnings.

In-Sample Evidence

Performance, risk, and factor loadings from the 1970–2003 formation period.

$1 invested in January 1970 grows to
$50.75
vs. $8.13 in the equal-weight benchmark — more than 6× the market's terminal wealth

Cumulative Growth of $1 — Strategy vs. Benchmark (1970–2003)

Max drawdown of −31.66% visible in 1973–74 oil crisis; value tilt protected against the 2000–02 dot-com bust.

12.92%
Annualized Return
0.80
Sharpe Ratio
−31.66%
Max Drawdown
+2.58%*
Alpha (FF6)

Performance Summary vs. Benchmark

Metric Strategy (1970–2003) Benchmark (EW) Difference
Annualized Return 12.92% 6.36% +6.56 pp
Volatility 16.22% 16.65% −0.43 pp
Sharpe Ratio 0.80 0.38 +0.42
Max Drawdown −31.66% −52.80% +21.14 pp shallower
Alpha (FF6) +2.58% * — —
Beta 0.8434 1.0 Lower market sensitivity
Takeaway: The strategy delivers more than twice the annualized return of the benchmark at lower volatility and with a max drawdown half as deep — a compelling risk-adjusted outcome in the formation period.

3-Year Rolling Sharpe Ratio: Strategy vs. Benchmark

Fama-French 6-Factor Regression — In-Sample

R² = 0.817  •  Adj. R² = 0.814  •  Factors significant at p < 0.10: Mkt-RF, HML, RMW, UMD

Factor Description Estimate t-value p-value Sig.
Mkt-RF Market excess return 0.9974 39.70 < 0.001 ***
HML Value (High Minus Low) 0.5429 10.33 < 0.001 ***
RMW Profitability 0.1228 2.73 0.007 **
UMD Momentum 0.0731 3.04 0.002 **
SMB Size (Small Minus Big) 0.0247 0.74 0.459 —
CMA Investment (Conservative vs. Aggressive) −0.0463 −0.62 0.539 —
Alpha (Intercept) +2.58% — — *
Interpretation: The portfolio loads strongly on value (HML) and profitability (RMW) — consistent with the factor selection. The significant momentum loading (UMD) confirms the ret_12_1 signal is capturing real momentum exposure. The significant alpha suggests performance beyond what these six factors explain.

Cumulative Growth of $1 — Long/Short Decile Spread by Factor

Each line = D10 minus D1 spread (sign-adjusted) | log scale. Confirms persistent factor premia in-sample.

Average Monthly Excess Return by Decile — In-Sample (1970–2003)

Stocks sorted into 10 buckets each month by each signal. Decile 1 = lowest signal value, Decile 10 = highest. Monotonic patterns confirm each factor's predictive validity in-sample.

Book-to-Market (be_me)
Net Income / Market (ni_me)
Momentum 12-1 (ret_12_1)
Operating Accruals (oaccruals — inverted in signal)
Accruals show a descending pattern by decile — confirming that lower accruals predict higher returns, which is why the signal is subtracted in the composite score.

Additional Performance Metrics

+17.11%
Best Month
−18.50%
Worst Month
60.5%
Months Beating Mkt
11 / 7
Win / Loss Streak
Best month: December 1975 (+17.11%) • Worst month: September 1987 (−18.50%, Black Monday adjacent) • Longest winning streak: 11 consecutive months • Longest losing streak: 7 consecutive months

Fama-MacBeth Cross-Sectional Significance

Factor Variable In Final Model? Note
Momentum ret_12_1 Yes — significant Strong cross-sectional predictor
Accruals oaccruals Yes — significant Inverted in composite signal
Profitability ni_me Yes — significant Earnings yield, inverse P/E
Value be_me Retained — marginal Not significant in regression but retained for theoretical motivation and portfolio diversification

Out-of-Sample Results

2004–2023: did the strategy hold up in the real world? How did performance change, and what does the factor model tell us?

$1 invested in January 2004 grows to
$5.43
vs. $4.19 in the benchmark — outperforming by $1.24 per dollar invested

Cumulative Growth of $1 — Strategy vs. Benchmark (2004–2023)

The 2008–09 financial crisis drove both portfolios to their maximum drawdowns (strategy −58.70%, benchmark −54.26%). Strategy outperforms by $1.24 over the 20-year period.

10.98%
OOS Ann. Return
0.56
OOS Sharpe Ratio
−58.70%
OOS Max Drawdown
+0.64%
Alpha (not sig.)

In-Sample vs. Out-of-Sample Comparison

Metric In-Sample (1970–2003) Out-of-Sample (2004–2023) Benchmark (OOS)
Annualized Return 12.92% 10.98% 8.91%
Volatility 16.22% 19.66% 16.06%
Sharpe Ratio 0.80 0.56 0.55
Max Drawdown −31.66% −58.70% −54.26%
Alpha (FF6) +2.58% * +0.64% (n.s.) —
Beta 0.8434 1.0685 1.0

Fama-French 6-Factor Regression — Out-of-Sample

R² = 0.790  •  Adj. R² = 0.784  •  Value + momentum loadings hold out-of-sample

Factor Description Estimate t-value p-value Sig.
Mkt-RF Market excess return 1.0757 23.63 < 0.001 ***
HML Value (High Minus Low) 0.5714 7.32 < 0.001 ***
UMD Momentum 0.2793 6.01 < 0.001 ***
RMW Profitability 0.1806 1.77 0.078 *
CMA Investment (Conservative vs. Aggressive) −0.2118 −1.75 0.081 —
SMB Size (Small Minus Big) 0.1263 1.53 0.128 —
Alpha (Intercept) +0.64% — — n.s.

Factor Loadings: In-Sample vs. Out-of-Sample

Notable shift: The momentum loading (UMD) grew from 0.07 to 0.28 out-of-sample, suggesting the momentum signal became more dominant in the post-2004 period. The CMA (investment) loading turned more negative, indicating the OOS portfolio tilts toward more aggressively-investing firms.

Average Monthly Excess Return by Decile — Out-of-Sample (2004–2023)

Same factor decile analysis applied to the OOS period. Factor spread patterns persist — confirming that the signals remain economically meaningful out-of-sample.

Book-to-Market (be_me)
Net Income / Market (ni_me)
Momentum 12-1 (ret_12_1)
Operating Accruals (oaccruals)
Factor decile spreads narrow slightly OOS (consistent with the weaker alpha), but the directional patterns hold — value, profitability, and momentum still show positive return gradients; accruals still shows a negative gradient.

OOS Impact Analysis

●

Alpha becomes insignificant OOS. The +2.58% in-sample alpha shrinks to +0.64% and loses statistical significance — consistent with some degree of in-sample overfitting or factor crowding over time.

●

Value and momentum loadings persist. HML (0.57) and UMD (0.28) remain highly significant OOS, validating that the strategy's core factor exposures are durable — not just in-sample artifacts.

●

Investment tilt (CMA) turns negative. The OOS portfolio exhibits an aggressive-investment tilt (negative CMA), meaning it holds companies that invest heavily. This wasn't present in-sample and may reflect post-2000 tech and growth concentration.

●

No size tilt (SMB near zero). Excluding micro-caps in portfolio construction successfully neutralizes size exposure — SMB is insignificant in both periods.

●

Market beta rises above 1.0. Beta climbs from 0.84 (IS) to 1.07 (OOS), increasing systematic market exposure. The strategy was more defensive in-sample than it proved to be going forward.

Full-Period Summary & Takeaways (1970–2023)

How the Keystone Quality Fund performed across the full 53-year horizon, combining the in-sample formation period and out-of-sample validation.

$275.33
Strategy Final Dollar
$200.23
Market Final Dollar
11.22%
Strategy CAGR
10.55%
Market CAGR

Full-Period Cumulative Growth: Strategy vs. Real-World Benchmarks (1970–2023)

Strategy CAGR: 11.22% | FF Total Market CAGR: ~10.3% | Equal-Weight Market: significantly lower. Dotted vertical line marks the IS/OOS split at 2004.

Investment Growth Calculator

Enter a hypothetical investment amount and select a period to see what the strategy would have returned vs. the market.

Would I Invest?

No — not in this form. Monthly rebalancing creates high transaction costs that would materially erode the 0.67% CAGR edge over the market. The strategy works as a research exercise, but real-world implementation requires accounting for bid-ask spreads, market impact, and taxes on realized gains. A quarterly or annual rebalance with a transaction cost filter would be a more realistic deployment.

Key Takeaways

+

Consistent outperformance: 63.9% monthly win rate and $75 of incremental terminal wealth over 53 years confirms the composite signal has real predictive power beyond noise.

+

Factor loadings hold out-of-sample: Value (HML) and momentum (UMD) remain highly significant OOS, meaning the economic rationale didn't evaporate after the formation period ended.

+

Drawdown protection in-sample: The strategy's max drawdown of −31.66% vs. the benchmark's −52.80% during the formation period demonstrates a meaningful risk-reduction benefit from the quality tilt.

−

Book-to-market not significant in Fama-MacBeth: be_me fails cross-sectional significance tests in both periods — likely due to overlap with ni_me and other value proxies, making it redundant rather than independent.

−

Possible in-sample overfitting: Variables were selected after observing in-sample results, creating look-ahead bias that likely inflates the formation-period Sharpe ratio and alpha estimate.

−

Blind to business quality: The model ranks purely by signal — it does not verify whether the underlying business is structurally sound. A cheap, deteriorating firm may screen well while being fundamentally broken.

−

Higher OOS market exposure: Beta rose from 0.84 in-sample to 1.07 out-of-sample. The strategy was more defensive in its formation period than it proved to be in live conditions — an important implementation risk.

Investment Philosophy Inspirations

The Keystone Quality Fund draws on decades of fundamental investing wisdom from two legendary practitioners — systematized into quantitative signals.

From Discretion to Data

Warren Buffett and Peter Lynch both made their fortunes buying quality businesses at reasonable prices. They didn't use statistical regressions — they read annual reports, walked factory floors, and trusted their judgment. This strategy asks: what if we could systematize their intuitions?

Each factor in the Keystone model is a quantitative proxy for a concept these investors described in their letters and interviews long before the academic literature formalized it.

Warren Buffett
Chairman, Berkshire Hathaway • ~19.8% CAGR (1965–2023) vs. S&P 500 ~10.2%

Background & Track Record

Born in Omaha, Nebraska in 1930, Buffett purchased his first stock at age 11 and filed his first tax return at 13. He studied under Benjamin Graham at Columbia Business School, absorbing the foundations of value investing, then ran Buffett Partnership Ltd. (1956–1969) with roughly 31% annual returns. He took control of Berkshire Hathaway in 1965 — then a failing textile mill — and transformed it into a diversified holding company. Over 58 years, Berkshire's per-share book value has compounded at ~19.8% annually versus the S&P 500's ~10.2%, producing one of the greatest long-run records in investment history.

Core Philosophy

Early on, Buffett followed Graham's "cigar butt" approach — buying deeply discounted assets regardless of business quality. Charlie Munger shifted his thinking: buy wonderful companies at fair prices rather than fair companies at wonderful prices. Durable competitive advantages — economic "moats" such as brand, switching costs, network effects, and scale — protect returns on capital over long periods against competitive erosion.

Key Principles

  • Intrinsic value: A business is worth the present value of its future free cash flows ("owner earnings"). Buy meaningfully below that figure.
  • Margin of safety: Always pay a sufficient discount to intrinsic value to protect against analytical errors and unforeseen events.
  • Economic moat: Durable advantages — brand loyalty, switching costs, network effects, low-cost structure — that prevent competitors from eroding returns on capital.
  • Management quality: Honest, capable, owner-oriented managers compound capital; poor managers destroy it regardless of the underlying business quality.
  • Long-term holding: "Our favorite holding period is forever." Low turnover eliminates friction and lets compounding do its work undisturbed.
  • Circle of competence: Only invest where you have a genuine edge; knowing the boundaries of what you understand is as important as what you know.

Connection to the Keystone Strategy

Buffett's emphasis on buying cheap relative to intrinsic value maps directly to:

be_me — Book-to-Market ni_me — Earnings Yield (inverse P/E)

His insistence on earnings quality — real cash earnings vs. accounting manipulation — is precisely what the accruals signal captures. Buffett's "owner earnings" concept strips out non-cash accruals to reveal true economic profit, mirroring Sloan (1996):

oaccruals — Cash-backed earnings quality

"Price is what you pay. Value is what you get." — Warren Buffett [2]

Peter Lynch
Former Manager, Fidelity Magellan Fund • 29.2% avg. annual return (1977–1990)

Background & Track Record

Lynch began caddying at a golf club frequented by Fidelity executives as a teenager — a chance encounter that led to a college internship and eventually a full-time research role. He took over the Magellan Fund in 1977 at age 33, when it had just $18 million in assets under management. By the time he retired in 1990 at age 46, Magellan held $14 billion and had posted a 29.2% average annual return, beating the S&P 500 in 11 of his 13 years. It was, at that point, the best-performing mutual fund in the world. His two books — One Up on Wall Street (1989) and Beating the Street (1993) — remain foundational reading for growth investors.

Core Philosophy

Lynch's framework is Growth at a Reasonable Price (GARP): find companies growing faster than the market expects, but don't overpay for that growth. He popularized "invest in what you know" — the idea that ordinary consumers identify great businesses before analysts do, simply by noticing what products they depend on. He was always hunting for "ten-baggers" — stocks that rise tenfold — hiding in plain sight.

Key Principles

  • PEG ratio: P/E divided by earnings growth rate. A PEG below 1.0 means you are paying less than the growth justifies — Lynch's quantified definition of "reasonable price."
  • Invest in what you know: Personal observation identifies great businesses before Wall Street does. Competitive edge comes from real-world insight, not just financial models.
  • Earnings growth is the engine: Long-term stock prices follow earnings trajectories. Find companies with consistent, underappreciated earnings growth that analysts have overlooked.
  • Story + numbers: Every investment needs a compelling two-minute thesis and data that confirms it — both must align before committing capital.
  • Stick with what's working: When a thesis plays out — fundamentals improving, stock price rising — stay the course. Lynch's biggest mistakes were selling winners too early.
  • Avoid hot tips and trendy names: Consensus ideas are already priced in. The real opportunity lies in neglected, misunderstood, or temporarily out-of-favor businesses.

Connection to the Keystone Strategy

Lynch's GARP discipline — buy profitable growth but don't overpay — is captured by:

ni_me — Earnings cheapness be_me — Asset cheapness

His observation that stocks with improving fundamentals tend to keep outperforming provided the intuition for the momentum factor — price momentum reflects earnings momentum:

ret_12_1 — 12-Month Price Momentum

"Know what you own, and know why you own it." — Peter Lynch [1]

Factor-to-Philosophy Mapping

Factor Variable Academic Source Lynch Connection Buffett Connection
Book-to-Market be_me Fama & French (1992) Don't overpay relative to assets Margin of safety; intrinsic value
Earnings Yield ni_me Novy-Marx (2013) PEG / GARP discipline Earnings power as intrinsic value anchor
Momentum ret_12_1 Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) Stay with working stories Less directly — Buffett is anti-momentum
Accruals (inverted) oaccruals Sloan (1996) Real vs. paper earnings Owner earnings; cash flow quality
Key distinction: Buffett and Lynch are concentrated, long-term, low-turnover investors. The Keystone strategy is diversified, short-horizon, high-turnover — it borrows their ideas about what to look for but not their implementation. This is a meaningful tension: the transaction costs of monthly rebalancing undermine the cost discipline both investors preached.

Sources & Citations

[1] Investopedia. "Peter Lynch." Investopedia. Available at: investopedia.com/terms/p/peterlynch.asp

[2] Investopedia. "Warren Buffett's Road to Riches." Investopedia, 2008. Available at: investopedia.com/articles/financial-theory/08/buffetts-road-to-riches.asp

[3] Lynch, P. & Rothchild, J. (1989). One Up on Wall Street. Simon & Schuster.

[4] Buffett, W. (Annual). Berkshire Hathaway Annual Shareholder Letters. Berkshire Hathaway Inc.

[5] Fama, E. & French, K. (1992). "The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns." Journal of Finance, 47(2), 427–465.

[6] Novy-Marx, R. (2013). "The Other Side of Value." Journal of Financial Economics, 108(1), 1–28.

[7] Sloan, R. (1996). "Do Stock Prices Fully Reflect Information in Accruals?" Accounting Review, 71(3), 289–315.

[8] Jegadeesh, N. & Titman, S. (1993). "Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers: Implications for Stock Market Efficiency." Journal of Finance, 48(1), 65–91.
